title: Dataset Formatting
lesson:
You have collected great examples and cleaned them. Now comes a quiet, unglamorous step that wrecks more fine-tuning runs than any other: dataset formatting — turning each example into the exact string of text and special markers the model expects to see during training.

Get this slightly wrong and nothing crashes. The training loss drops, the charts look fine, and yet your model comes out subtly broken. Formatting is the plumbing: invisible when right, a flood in the basement when wrong.

Why the wrapping matters so much

A model does not see role and content fields. It only ever sees a flat sequence of tokens (the little chunks of text a model reads and writes). So your tidy structured example has to be flattened into one long string, with markers telling the model "the user is talking now" or "the assistant's turn starts here."

Those markers are special tokens — reserved symbols like <|im_start|> or [INST] that aren't normal words; they're signposts. The recipe that decides exactly where each marker goes is the chat template.

Think of it like addressing an envelope. The letter inside (your content) can be perfect, but if you skip the postcode, smudge the house number, or use the wrong country's format, it never arrives. Every model family has its own envelope format. A letter addressed the Llama way will not be delivered correctly to a Qwen, and vice versa.

The single golden rule: match the model's own template

Each base model was pretrained or instruction-tuned with one specific template. You must format your fine-tuning data the same way, down to the last newline. Luckily you rarely hand-write this: the model ships with its template baked into its tokenizer, and libraries apply it for you.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-8B")

messages = [
  {"role": "system", "content": "You are concise."},
  {"role": "user", "content": "Capital of Japan?"},
  {"role": "assistant", "content": "Tokyo."},
]
text = tok.apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False)
print(text)

That one call (apply_chat_template) reads the model's official recipe and inserts every special token in the right spot. The output might look like this — notice the signposts wrapping each turn:

<|im_start|>system
You are concise.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
Capital of Japan?<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant
Tokyo.<|im_end|>

Loss masking: grade only the answer

Here is the second half of formatting, and the part beginners miss. During SFT, the model should only be graded on the assistant's reply — not on the system prompt or the user's question. The setting that controls this is the loss mask (loss is the score that measures how wrong the model's guesses are; the mask says which tokens count).

If the mask is wrong, the model learns to predict the question instead of the answer. It is like a chef who memorises the customers' orders word-for-word but never learns to cook the dish.

Most training frameworks have a flag for this — Axolotl calls it train_on_inputs: false, and TRL's chat trainers mask the prompt by default. Always confirm it is on. Tools like Unsloth, Axolotl, and the TRL Library handle templating and masking together — exactly why people use them instead of stitching strings by hand.

Storage format vs prompt format

Two different things both get called "format," so let's separate them:

  • The file format is how rows sit on disk — almost always JSONL (one JSON object per line).
  • The prompt format is the special-token wrapping shown above.

You store clean messages lists in JSONL and let the template add the wrapping at training time. Don't pre-bake special tokens into your stored data, or you'll double them up when the trainer adds its own.

File format (on disk)Prompt format (what the model sees)
JSONL rows of messagesFlat string with special tokens
Human-readable, easy to diffModel-specific, fragile
You write and store thisThe chat template generates this
Same across modelsDifferent per model family

One more analogy: a chat template is like sheet music. The notes (your words) are the same idea, but each orchestra (model family) reads them in its own notation. Hand a violinist guitar tablature and you get noise, not music.

So the workflow is simple: store clean JSONL, apply the correct template, verify the loss mask, and always print one fully-rendered example before you spend money on a long run. This pairs directly with Tokenization and Dataset Cleaning.

⚠️ The most expensive bug in fine-tuning is a wrong template that runs silently for hours. Print one rendered example and eyeball the special tokens before launching.
mentalModel: Your stored data is a clean letter; the chat template is the envelope each model demands. Flatten the letter into the model's exact special-token wrapping, mask everything except the assistant's reply, and always read one finished envelope out loud before mailing 50,000 of them.
takeaways: Store clean JSONL messages; let the model's chat template add special tokens.
Match the model's exact template, down to the last newline.
Mask the loss so only the assistant's reply is graded.

diagram.caption: Clean JSONL messages run through the model's chat template into a flat token string; only the assistant reply is graded, and you print one rendered row to verify before training.

mistakes: Using the wrong model's template. Formatting Qwen data with Llama tokens (or any mismatch) trains the model on signposts it never learned. It often still produces low loss, so you won't notice until the outputs are mangled. Always pull the template from the exact model you're fine-tuning.

Forgetting the loss mask. If training grades the prompt too (e.g. train_on_inputs: true by accident), the model learns to echo questions instead of answering. Only the assistant tokens should count — confirm the mask before every run.

Double-adding special tokens. Pre-baking <|im_start|> markers into your stored JSONL and letting the trainer apply a template gives you doubled tokens, which the model has never seen. Store plain messages; let the template do the wrapping once.

Dropping the EOS / end-of-turn token. If the example never ends with the model's stop signal (like <|im_end|> or EOS), the model never learns to stop talking, and at inference it rambles forever. Make sure each assistant turn is properly closed.

Whitespace and newline drift. Templates are picky about exact spaces and line breaks. A stray trailing space or a missing newline counts as a different token sequence. Use apply_chat_template rather than hand-building strings, so the spacing is exact.

Never printing a rendered example. The cheapest insurance in all of fine-tuning is one print(text) on a single formatted row. Skipping it is how a silent template bug burns an entire GPU-day — see Data Curation.
exercise:
Take any open instruction model on Hugging Face (e.g. a small Qwen or Llama). Load its tokenizer and write a 3-message conversation (system, user, assistant). Call apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False) and print the result. Find and label every special token in the output: where does each turn start, and how does it end? Then re-run it for a different model family and place the two rendered strings side by side. List three concrete differences in their markers.

Stretch goal: Tokenize the rendered string with tokenize=True and build the matching loss mask by hand — set the label for every system and user token to the "ignore" value (commonly -100) and keep the assistant tokens. Verify that the number of kept tokens equals the length of just the assistant reply plus its end-of-turn marker.
solution: Hint: For Qwen-style models you'll see <|im_start|>role opening each turn and <|im_end|> closing it; Llama-3 family uses <|start_header_id|>role<|end_header_id|> then <|eot_id|>. Three differences will jump out: the start/end marker spelling, whether the role sits on its own line, and the leading system token (Llama adds a <|begin_of_text|>; Qwen does not). For the loss mask: render with add_generation_prompt=False, find the index where the assistant content begins, set every earlier label to -100, and the count of non--100 labels should match the assistant text plus its closing token. If your kept count includes the user's words, your mask is off by exactly the prompt length — the classic bug.
summary: Dataset formatting flattens your clean examples into the precise token sequence a model expects. Store rows as JSONL messages, then apply the model's own chat template to add special tokens (signposts marking each turn), and mask the loss so only the assistant reply is graded. Match the exact template, close every turn with its stop token, never double-wrap, and always print one rendered example before a long run.

keyTerms.def: Turning structured examples into the exact text and special tokens a specific model expects to read during training.
A model's built-in recipe that flattens a messages list into one string, inserting the right special tokens for each turn.
Reserved markers (like <|im_start|> or [INST]) that aren't normal words but act as signposts telling the model whose turn it is.
The tokenizer function that applies a model's official chat template so you don't have to assemble the special tokens by hand.
JSON Lines: a file with one complete JSON object per line, the standard on-disk format for training datasets.
The setting that tells training which tokens to grade. For SFT it should cover only the assistant reply, not the prompt.
The special stop signal that marks where a turn ends. Without it the model never learns to stop generating.
File format is how rows sit on disk (JSONL); prompt format is the special-token wrapping the model actually sees.
A formatting bug that doesn't crash and even shows good-looking loss, but quietly trains a broken model.

quiz.q: What is the single most important rule when formatting data to fine-tune a specific model?
options: Always use JSON instead of JSONL on disk
Match that model's own chat template exactly, down to the newlines
Pick whichever template produces the lowest training loss
Add as many special tokens as possible for safety
quiz.explain: Each model learned one specific template; using a different one trains it on signposts it never saw. Low loss is no proof the template is right — it can drop even when formatting is wrong.

quiz.q: During SFT, which tokens should the loss actually be computed on?
options: Every token in the conversation equally
Only the system prompt and user question
Only the assistant's reply (and its end-of-turn token)
quiz.explain: You want the model to learn to produce the answer, not to memorise the prompt. Masking everything except the assistant reply is what teaches it to respond rather than echo the question.

quiz.q: You store rows as plain `messages` in JSONL AND also paste <|im_start|> markers into the content yourself. What goes wrong?
options: Nothing — extra markers are harmless redundancy
The trainer applies the template too, so special tokens get doubled
The file stops being valid JSONL
The loss mask automatically fixes the duplicates
quiz.explain: The chat template adds its own special tokens at training time, so pre-baking them produces doubled markers the model never saw. Store clean messages and let the template wrap them exactly once.

quiz.q: Your run finished with a nice low loss curve, but the fine-tuned model rambles and never stops. What's the most likely formatting cause?
options: The dataset was saved as JSONL instead of CSV
The examples were missing the EOS / end-of-turn token
There were too few examples in the dataset
The learning rate was set too high
quiz.explain: Without the stop token closing each assistant turn, the model never learns where a reply ends, so it generates forever at inference. A clean loss curve can hide this because the bug is silent.
